title: Mem0: Building Production-Ready AI Agents with Scalable Long-Term Memory
summary: This paper introduces Mem0 and Mem0g, two memory architectures designed to overcome the fixed context window limitation of LLMs for long-term multi-session dialogues. Mem0 incrementally extracts, updates, and retrieves salient facts from conversations using a vector database and LLM-driven operations (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP). Mem0g extends this with a directed labeled graph (entities as nodes, relationships as edges) stored in Neo4j, enabling explicit relational reasoning. Evaluated on the LOCOMO benchmark, both variants outperform six baseline categories (including LoCoMo, MemGPT, RAG, Zep, OpenAI memory) across single-hop, multi-hop, temporal, and open-domain questions. Mem0 achieves 91% lower p95 latency and >90% token savings vs. full-context processing, while delivering 26% relative improvement in LLM-as-a-Judge scores over OpenAI's memory system.
key_insights: **Two-phase incremental ingestion pipeline**: Process message pairs with dual context (conversation summary + recent message history) to extract candidate memories. This design prevents redundant re-processing of entire histories and enables real-time updates during ongoing conversations.

**Four operation memory update mechanism**: Use an LLM with function calling to classify each extracted fact as ADD (new), UPDATE (augment existing), DELETE (contradiction), or NOOP (redundant). This is a practical policy for maintaining a consistent, non-redundant, and temporally coherent knowledge base without manual rules.

**Graph-based memory for temporal reasoning**: Represent memories as directed labeled graphs (entities as nodes, relationships as edges) with timestamps and semantic embeddings per node. This structure gives significant gains on temporal questions (e.g., "when did X happen?") by making event sequences and durations explicit. Combine entity-centric retrieval (anchor nodes + expand relationships) with semantic triplet matching.

**Conflict detection with soft deletion**: When new information contradicts existing graph edges, mark old edges as invalid rather than physically deleting them. This preserves the full temporal record and enables the system to reason about belief changes over time.

**Efficient retrieval via selective memory access**: Instead of retrieving large chunks of raw text (as in RAG) or passing full conversation history, retrieve only a small set of semantically similar memories (e.g., top-10 facts). This yields 91% lower p95 latency and >90% token cost savings vs. full-context, while maintaining competitive accuracy.

**Asynchronous summary generation**: Maintain a periodically updated conversation summary separate from the main processing pipeline. This provides global thematic context for memory extraction without blocking ingestion, balancing freshness with latency.

**Practical trade-offs between fact density and graph structure**: Base Mem0 (dense natural language facts) works best for single-hop and multi-hop queries. Mem0g (graph representation) excels at temporal and open-domain reasoning but adds moderate latency and token overhead. Choose representation based on query-type distribution in your application.
